key_insight: A reduced-basis hybrid TBNN-UDE framework can learn frame-invariant viscoelastic constitutive laws from shear data and transfer them to more complex flow regimes.
engineering_relevance: This enables data-efficient digital twins for viscoelastic fluids that can be embedded directly into CFD solvers for predictive simulation of industrial flows.
method: Combine Tensor Basis Neural Networks with Universal Differential Equations to represent nonlinear constitutive behavior.
Use a reduced tensor-basis formulation to improve physical consistency and numerical stability.
Train on synthetic oscillatory-shear datasets and deploy the learned model at runtime in an open-source finite-volume CFD solver.
evidence_signal: The models were trained on synthetic viscoelastic data from oscillatory shear and then tested by extrapolation to extensional flows and 2D/3D benchmarks such as sudden contraction and cross-slot cases.
watch_item: Accuracy drops as extrapolation increases, so performance and robustness should be validated carefully before using the model outside the training regime.
paper_title: Harnessing Machine Learning for Hybrid Constitutive Modelling of Viscoelastic Fluid Flows in Computational Rheology
